comment_text:
Some people here think that Usagi's decision to go back was random or out of character. But I don't think Usagi's decision to go to the Borderlands again was random. 

When I rewatched the series again, there were scenes of Usagi grieving her father's death and that still stayed with her throughout the series. She also had a flashback when her father told her to live her life even if he's gonna d*e, that just doesn't sit right for her and to even us, the audience. 

Also, keep in mind that she forgot about her time in the Borderlands already. Then she was manipulated by Ryuji, thinking she's gonna meet her father in Borderlands since he's always in her mind like haunting her (meaning he hasn't passed on to heaven yet or reincarnated whatever you believe). She didn't know Ryuji's full background, or that if she agreed, he would pull her back to the Borderlands. 

So, Usagi's decision was not random at all. She has unresolved issues, she doesn't want to open it up to Arisu since she is living a happy life like what her father wants her to have. But still want to have closure for her father's d*ath.

video_title: Alice In Borderland Season 3 | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
SPOILER AHEAD:



Usagi wasn’t tricked — she willingly took a drug that would stop her heart just to see her father one more time.

As a filmmaker, I’ve got huge respect for the masterful suspense, the production quality, and the design of the games this season. Technically, it’s outstanding — easily one of Netflix’s best-made shows.

But thematically, it felt forced and frustrating. A married woman, who vowed to stand by her husband “in sickness and in health,” choosing to take a potentially lethal drug to process her trauma — without thinking of him — just felt repellent and far-fetched to me.

Brilliant execution, but that moral choice really pulled me out of it.

comment_text:
What Happened? Season 3 Completely Lost the Plot
Honestly, the first two seasons of Alice in Borderland were incredible. They were so smart, so intense-easily an 8/10. The games were genius, and I was completely invested in the characters. It was a benchmark for the whole survival thriller genre.

But Season 3? It's baffling. It feels like whoever took over the writing just didn't know where to go with the story. The plot feels forcefully pushed and completely directionless, as if they desperately needed a reason to drag the characters back into the Borderland.

It's a chaotic mess that's unsatisfying from start to finish. I'm shocked by the massive drop in quality; it's a complete disaster compared to the brilliance that came before it. This season barely deserves a 2/10. It's a real shame to see a great show end up like this.

comment_text:
Mischaracterization of Usagi

I know Usagi gets a lot of hate this season (and I understand where the hate is coming from) but this season is written purely out of netflix’s ass, and butchered Usagi’s character so bad.

Wether you read the manga or watched the show (especially S1) you ought to remember Usagi is NOT an emotional person whatsoever. She’s a climber making her very practical. 
In the manga, Usagi was bullied. And watched her father hang himself right in front of her eyes. So she’s not someone easily broken if you may say.  

Also, in the show and manga, Usagi’s dad died at least a year before the current events take place. Adding the five years she spent with Arisu, that makes him dead for 6 years. Why would she all of a sudden get super emotional about him? Her decision to endanger herself, trust her life with a stranger, and pull Arisu in with her (because did she think he will leave her?) is extremely selfish and out of character. 

Usagi doesn’t act based on her emotions, rather just pure logic. She was the one keeping Arisu in check multiple times over his breakdowns. They literally became close because she saved him from dying, telling him that just because his friends died doesn’t mean he has to. In what world would someone like that (who spoke such words to Arisu like a day after his friends died) risk her life for the same reason? 

I don’t get why they couldn’t just adapt the damn manga or at the very least let Aso write the season himself. Anyhow, read the manga and be prepared for a new Usagi.

comment_text:
Just finished watching Season 3 about half an hour ago, and I wanted to share some thoughts with the community.
First of all, I think S3 was a banger. I know people are comparing it to S1 and S2, but honestly, it doesn’t need to be compared—each season has its own strengths. The storyline this time felt refreshing, the new characters added a lot, and Arisu + Usagi are still the emotional core of the show.
The games were super creative, the cinematography was beautiful, and the pacing kept me hooked. And let’s be real: after they teased the Joker card at the end of S2, they had to make S3. It really tied things together well.
That said, there were definitely some loopholes that bugged me. Biggest one: why did Usagi agree to return to the Borderlands with Ryuijin just to meet her dead father? Like… girl, you had a peaceful life with Arisu 😭🙏 That decision felt so random and out of character.
Still, overall, I really enjoyed this season. IMO it’s actually better than Squid Game. I’d give it a 5/5 and will probably rewatch it soon.

comment_text: I can't believe i am saying this but Season 3 was very lackluster, and i don't usually drop comment on Youtube. Sure the games are cool but there are some plot holes that bother me until the very end, like why this guy can travel between worlds and hunt the survivors, it's not explained well until the end. And the overall pacing feels very rushed since it's only 6 episodes. The new side characters aren't well developed unlike the first two seasons. Again, pacing problem. Also, it couldn't have been more obvious that this season is very Squid Game Season 3-ified, and no it's not only because of the game concept thing, watch what happens in the last game and also the last scene. It's way too similar. And i truly don't understand what's psychological about the last game... You just need to find the room, why are some random imaginary scenes on a projector affecting you that much. The whole season feels too underwhelming. It started nice but fell flat, could have been better.

Hello world. This is Morocco — not the glossy postcards you see, but the hidden truth. Families who lost everything in the 2023 earthquake are still living in tents, waiting for homes that never come. Hospitals are overcrowded and without medicine, mothers wait for hours only to be told there is nothing for their children. Schools are packed with too many students and too few teachers, leaving children with diplomas that mean nothing because there are no jobs. Entire neighborhoods are destroyed to build stadiums and luxury projects, while the poor are pushed out of their houses with nowhere to go. Young people who raise their voices are silenced, beaten, or jailed, their dreams crushed before they are born. Children are forced to work in the streets instead of learning in classrooms, some disappearing into places where no one asks questions. Poverty is everywhere — fathers with empty hands, mothers crying because they cannot feed their families, youth staring at a future that does not exist. This is the Morocco that lives in silence, the Morocco the world does not see, the Morocco that deserves justice, dignity, and hope.

comment_text: It's increasingly clear that these worlds are elaborate man-made simulations, likely set in the distant future, created either for scientific study, entertainment, or both. The characters within them are not mere artificial constructs, but transferred consciousnesses of real beings, repurposed to inhabit these synthetic realities. Trapped in endlessly looping scenarios that shift and evolve, they are powerless to break free or halt the cycle. Their existence is condemned to perpetual repetition, continuing until the simulation either crashes, is deliberately shut down, or collapses by some unforeseen failure.

comment_text:
...I mean, I still rewatch the first 2 seasons with new people I can talk into giving the series a shot lol... it's just such a well-rounded story as it is already, with the genius ending in season 2, and everyone I watched it with also ended up fascinated by the show! ...but also content with how it unfolded.
...but as a fan, no matter how much I appreciate the original 2-season-long storyline, I just CANNOT complain about MORE of the good stuff, like?? Sure, give me a season 3!! I might not treat it as canon in my head, but even a slightly 💩ier Alice in Borderland is better than NO more AiB ever! (I'll never understand fans of certain shows/movies complaining about getting MORE stuff to watch, like??) The original still remains the masterpiece it is, and you get some additional stories with the same great characters in the same awesome world, why not...?
Alice in Borderland, I'll def be tuning in!!
